speaker: interviewer
text: This is Tape 7.

speaker: interviewee
text: Suddenly, there was a tremendous amount of noise-- banging, shouting, screaming. A man's voice. And she-- poor lady over there-- thinking, yes, well it's the gestapo who come to collect us or whatever, or to kill us. And she always said, well, if anything like this happens, I'm going onto the balcony, I throw myself down. I'm not letting myself into their hands. I'm not letting them catch me alive. And so she started going onto the balcony. But he-- you open the door, [INAUDIBLE]. And she suddenly recognized his voice. And that was the owner of the flat. [? Sapina ?] [INAUDIBLE], [? Sappy. ?] Who suddenly decided to return. He decided, because somebody, some very nice friend of hers-- who was in correspondence with him, were friendly together and so on-- decided to tell him that, while you are away, doing whatever you are doing there in Holland, she's having a very nice time. She has a very nice boyfriend here and enjoys life. So you better, you know, do something about it. He didn't waste any time and arrived without announcing himself. Nothing. Just came like a police dog under the dog wire, quietly like a dog, and the door wanted to open, because he had the keys. And he was opening the door. And luckily, luckily, God was with us again. And she-- the door was on the train, so he couldn't get in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it went open only a little bit and no more. He started screaming and shouting. That's when she recognized his voice. And what is she to do now, my god? But she went and opened the door. And that moment, he started like a madman, running around the flat shooting the gun, shooting. All over the place he was shooting-- under the bed, in the bed, inside, in the cupboards. Everywhere, shooting and shouting, where is your so and so? And where is your-- using very foul language. Where is [INAUDIBLE] you know. She had no idea why he-- what it was all about, because she did have somebody, it's true, some time ago. But this was a Wehrmacht officer, and he used to come once a week, spend a night and went. And he was very good, because he was a gentleman in a way. He never, ever went wherever he wasn't asked to come. I mean, he came, went into the dining room, went into the bedroom, went into the bathroom, and went home. Right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kitchen was not his territory. So she could go in, she could go out. He didn't bother us, so to speak. He was quite safe for us, because we knew that he would not touch upon us here. And he always left very early in the morning. And that was all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, he gave her an ultimatum-- an ultimatum one fine day-- and said, look. It's either the garlic or it's me. Simple as that. And she said, what do you mean? He said, look, I can't take this anymore. I can't smell this anymore. So, you know, which is it? She said, well, I'm sorry. But if I have to make a choice, it's the garlic. So off he went, and that was that. And we stayed with the garlic. I was chopping up the garlic every day so she could have two spoonfuls of garlic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our Savior saved our lives, undoubtedly. This-- whatever habit of hers, or this incredible persistence or phobia. This absolute obsession that this is what is-- this is better than a doctor, better than any medicine. Finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so he went off. In the meantime, the other one was informed that there was such a man, and he comes on Wednesdays. And so he knew it all. And he came on Wednesday to Thursday morning, at 6 o'clock in the morning. But he wasn't there. Because by that time, it was either the garlic or me, so goodbye. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: And shooting all over the place, running like absolutely in a fit of hysteria. And all over-- bathroom, bedroom, toilet, everything. Now he comes into the kitchen, and we are in this gallery. And we, in our fervor together, locked ourselves in, but didn't take the key out. Silly, I mean, but we didn't-- and then, now, we don't dare to take the key out. He comes into the kitchen, and we think now this is it, goodbye. Because our hearts were beating so that you could hear it from here to Olympia. Every single heart beat like that. And for him not to hear it-- not to hear it, let alone do anything. I mean, he must have been made deaf there and then. He comes, he shot everywhere. He shot every wardrobe. He shot into every receptacle. He stands by our door. He tries to open it. It's locked. The keys inside the door, he doesn't look. He doesn't shoot. He turns and goes away. She, in the meantime, is standing on the balcony, because she thinks, when I hear four shots, I throw myself. But there were no four shots. He stayed for two weeks with us there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now he noticed-- he realized whatever happened, and she persuaded him it wasn't true, God knows what, and thinking that he would go away. But he said, no, I took a furlough, two weeks furlough. I'm staying, and aren't you pleased? She says yes, I'm quite delighted. What could she do? She was in his flat, in his apartment. Can you imagine that poor woman at that moment? Here we are, the four of us, in this gallery, he with her there, and he's staying. And she has no means of communicating, telling us anything. So she said to him, tell me, where is your luggage? Did you come-- [INAUDIBLE] said, no, I left it at the station. So she says, don't you want to go and get your luggage? Go and get your luggage! You can't be just like that. He said, oh, that's a good idea. I'll go and get my luggage. And he goes and gets his luggage. And in the meantime, she tells us about the arrangement. And we have to-- we, I mean not I, because [INAUDIBLE] what to do. I mean, to go? Where do we go? We have nowhere to go. Into the Street We have nowhere to go. So suddenly, they have an idea. That the wife-- this lady who came to keep me company, she is going to be the maid, the servant. My mother is going to be her dressmaker. And they're coming and spending the whole day doing the job for her. And then in the evening, they go home, and they come first thing in the morning. In other words, they let themselves out, knock-- close the door, bang the door very loudly and ostentatiously. And immediately, into the kitchen, and into the scullery.

speaker: interviewee
text: The man and I sit in the scullery all day long. For two weeks we're like this. My mother had a machine-- sewing machine, brought by her. It was delivered at that time. She asked for a sewing machine to be delivered at home, and the machine was delivered. Was put against the scullery door. And there was my mother sitting against the scullery door in the kitchen sewing for her. The other one was cleaning, cooking, doing things, everything. And that was perfectly natural and normal to him. But it should be so. It wasn't anything extraordinary. They didn't understand one word of German. They didn't speak one word of German, of course. You know, because they are local. They don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the man, [INAUDIBLE], in this gallery. For two weeks, he was coming into-- and this particular [? sappy, ?] he loved cooking. He was a sort of house man. He liked to do things about the house. He wasn't one to sit put, stay put and be served. He liked to come and experiment and do things. He spent all days in the kitchen. She had to go to work normally, and he had nothing to do. He was there with us. Can you imagine the situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: And as [INAUDIBLE], he was. And he was telling them tall stories, amazing stories of how Germany's winning on all fronts, on all fronts. In Africa, ah, they're beating. And, of course, they're bobbing London to bits. And there won't be anything left of England by the time they finish with it. And, of course, Russia, on the Russian front, nothing. The Russians are all the way back to God knows where. And then they're beating them everywhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, you know, occasionally, he had an idea to have a little pinch here and there. It was terrible. And we were sitting, and my mother was dying that I shouldn't laugh out loud, because sometimes it was very funny, you know? I mean, the things he was saying, thinking that-- trying to make them understand how it was-- and, of course, they understood every word. They spoke very well German but pretended not. The way they spoke German to him was very, very, very pigeoned.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so my mother was frightened that I may burst out laughing. One can sneeze, one can cough. Anything can happen. It was just absolutely terrifying. And there we were with him for two weeks furlough. Don't ask me how we survived, but we did. We did. And he decided, after two weeks, that he needs to go back. And he went back, and we went to our old routines, so to speak. But it was-- it was something.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, every night, we had to go and sleep on the floor, the four of us. And he with her over there. Now, what if he would like to have a glass of water from the kitchen? If he goes to the toilet-- the toilet was near the kitchen. There was a window high up, but still there was a--

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, it was-- I don't today how [INAUDIBLE], how we managed.

speaker: interviewer
text: If he was so interested in cooking, he might have wanted to go into the scullery?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anything. He might have wanted anything. She had to keep him occupied, believe me, all the time. All the time. Not let him think about anything else. That was not easy. Unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: In any event, then the frown started. The changing and the Russians started coming closer and closer. And the Germans-- the Germans, particularly the civilian personnel, was asked to leave within two weeks that they have to leave [INAUDIBLE] and go back to Germany. In other words, they were being sent back home. And now what do we do? This man, who was with us-- the couple, who were with my mother and me-- they had a place to go to. They had everything prepared. First of all, they had cyanide on them. And way beforehand, [INAUDIBLE] said to him, when he told her that he had cyanide, she said to him, right, do you? For how many people? For two. She said, well, this is not good enough. You've got to have it for four. You have to share it out. I don't have. So she said, you have to get it. I don't get. There isn't such a thing as for two here or for one. You are four, and whatever happens to one happens to four, and to four to one. She. He provided. He was a chemist, don't forget, an apothecary. He provided cyanide for the four of us. Then when it came to finding a place where to go to, he said, please don't worry about my wife and me. We have work to go to. So she said to him, oh do you really? And what do you think is going to happen to [INAUDIBLE] and to [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know, he said. I don't know. She said, well, you better find out very quickly that they can go too. Because if they don't go, you won't go. I won't let you go. And he said, I can't do anything. I mean, this was prepared for us, and this was, you know. And she said, I don't care what. You are here only thanks this little girl here, she says, pointing at me. And don't you ever forget it. And you are not going to go now and save your own skin. You are going to do something for these two ladies now. Otherwise, you will not go, I promise you. And he had to, and he did. And then, thanks to him, we were taken to the Greek Catholic convent. Actually, the metropolitan, the head of the Greek Catholic church, took us in-- all of us, at first. And then he separated us here and there. And my mother and me, we were in a convent and an orphanage at the same time, a Ukrainian one. And for me, it wasn't such a terrible thing, because I spoke Russian. And for me to learn Ukrainian was not difficult, and I did very quickly. But for my mother, it was very difficult. She could never, never, never learn that language. And so they decided that she was going to be a deaf and numb person. In other words, doesn't speak at all. Doesn't hear, doesn't speak. Fine. [INAUDIBLE]. Well, easier said than done. It was very difficult for her to play such a role. I mean, constantly to not to react, you know, to whatever. And what she did-- and she was as such. And I was in the orphanage, and she was in the actual convent, in the quarters of the Reverend mother, Mother Superior. She kept her in her quarters. She provided for her the necessary papers and genuine papers-- birth certificates and such like and so on. I had nothing-- absolutely nothing, no papers whatsoever. Again. But-- well, if anybody had asked, it would have been very bad. But thank God, nobody did. And there we were until we were, so to speak, liberated again by the Russians, which was in July of 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were-- what should I say? I mean, there were some very difficult moments there. We were exposed enormously, and they were exposed enormously. Because the metropolitan had a considerable amount of Jewish people. This happened-- they say that an event sort of triggered that off, that at first he was not going to help, and he didn't help. So the story goes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I will not swear by it. I don't know. But the moment after the so-called-- the August 1942 selection, when my father and my little brother died, the chief rabbi of Lwow, who was also a friend, a personal friend of the Metropolitan, came to him, asking him to help. To open up whatever he can to take in people to save them from death and so forth. And the Metropolitan apparently said, I cannot do that. I can only take you and your family. I'll do that, but I can't take everybody. And he apparently said, well, I haven't come to plead for my family and for myself. I came to plead for my people. If you can't do it, I'm sorry, and goodbye, and went out. And as he went out, he was shot dead by the Ukrainian militiamen. And from that moment on, the Metropolitan said, whoever will come to me, I'll give him haven. I'll take him in.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you hear that? And how did you know that that had happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is how the story goes. I mean, I know the sons of Rabbi [? Levin. ?] And I came with one of them to this country. And that's what-- that's how we knew the story to be in Lwow, you know. That's how they-- it went by grape-vine. Whether it happened exactly that way, I don't know. They say that this is what triggered the fact that the Metropolitan opened up his doors and people came. And then he indeed saved a considerable number of children and some families. I mean, this [INAUDIBLE], who was with me, he, his wife, and his son. His son, who today is a very prominent professor of dermatology. Sorry, not dermatology, of laryngology in Israel. A prominent professor with his own clinic. I mean, these people survived. A unit, a complete family unit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where had the son been before?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the Metropolitan. He had him already there. The [INAUDIBLE] had him there already. And they had a place prepared. You see, he was delivering medicines, medications, to the palace of the Metropolitan before the war even. That's how he knew him. And so he arranged, and then he promised him, all right, when you need to, and you absolutely have nowhere else, I'll help you.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's understandable that he couldn't take everyone. It's understandable. I mean, you know. On the other hand, on the other hand, to say no is equally bad. But from that moment on, as the story goes, he did help.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was Doctor-- Rabbi Dr. [? Kahana, ?] who, with his wife and little girl, and the wife highly pregnant, he had them. He had us, and he had a number of other young people, children, some not so young. He saved a lot of people. We are now trying desperately that he's acknowledged, but there are some difficulties that way. However, there it was. And there we managed to survive the war. And for us, it finished in July '44 when the Russians came in. We stayed in Lwow for approximately a year, my mother and I, because we were always, always hoping-- looking for, hoping for some shred of evidence about my father. That maybe, just possibly, maybe the child is somewhere alive. Maybe the-- I mean, people were coming back from hiding, from here, from there. There were very few people left in Lwow. We had this-- as I said, this wonderful assistance of the local population. And they were both Poles and Ukrainians. So, you know, very few survived. But there were those locals who helped too. So for about a year, there we were. And then we decided we're not going to stay. We don't want to stay in Russia or be there, and we're going back to Krakow. And we did. We went back. It took us about a week to get to Krakow on a train, in a cattle train. It took six days from Lwow to get to Krakow in a cattle train. Crowded, in the heat of the summer. Unbearable. Unbearable. Such a journey we had. And God knows why it took so long, but it was standing for days on end at some station or something. And then we arrived in Krakow, and we had to change our names. We couldn't be, again, under our own name, because we couldn't even get a room if we were under the name Stern. No, no. People didn't want to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to change our names to some less Jewish sounding name. And my mother again was working and, you know, sewing and doing so much-- as much as she could to keep us overboard. And again, every day in the Polish Jewish, so to speak, committee formed itself. And there was a house, and everybody gathered there. Everybody came looking for someone. Every day there was scenes, incredible scenes when somebody of the family found somebody of a family, they found each other. It could have been the father, it could have been the cousin, it could have been the brother. It was incredible scenes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly one day, I learned that I was going to go to London. Just like that. Didn't do anything in the direction. Nothing. I learnt I was going to London. In fact, Dr. Kahana-- this particular rabbi, Dr. [? Kahana, ?] who was by that time the chief rabbi of the Polish forces in Warsaw, put me on a list of children. Groups were being formed. There was a man by the name of Rabbi Dr. [INAUDIBLE] here in London. And this man was a walking miracle. This man was something so remarkable, what achievements for him are-- I mean, so many that one cannot. But those who know who he was will know who I'm talking about. And he decided that he's going to bring out children from after-- aftermath of war from Poland, take them out of there and give them a chance in life. And so he brought out three transports of children after the war, not to mention the transport of children he brought before the war, but I'm not talking about that. But after the war. And he was in touch with the Rabbi Dr. [? Kahana, ?] who was his go-between, if you like, on the other side. And there was a whole organization buying out children. People didn't want to part with them. Some people didn't want to give them back. Some people wanted to give them back only if they were going to be paid adequately. There were all sorts of dramatic situations, really lots of dramas. And [PAUSES FOR 62 SECONDS]
